# **Architecture Note - IS2209 Group 43**

## **Context Diagram**

The Dog Dashboard is a Flask-based web application that uses three services: The Dog API, Supabase (PostgreSQL), and Render. Users interact with the application through a web browser, which communicates with the Flask backend. The backend makes requests to The Dog API to retrieve dog images and breed information, reads and writes to a Supabase PostgreSQL database to log requests and store favourites, and is hosted using Render.

## **Integration Points**![](data:image/png;base64,)

The application has three main integration points.

### **The Dog API**

The Dog API is an external API that provides dog images and breed information. We connect to it by sending requests from our Flask app using Python's requests library. To authenticate, our API key is stored as a .env variable and included in the header of each request. We use two main endpoints; one to search for dog images, either randomly or filtered by breed, and another to get detailed breed information such as temperament and lifespan. We added graceful error handling around these calls using try/except blocks so that if the API is unavailable, the app returns a helpful error message rather than crashing.

### **Supabase**

Supabase provides a hosted PostgreSQL database. The application connects using psycopg2 with a DATABASE_URL .env variable. Two tables are used: dog_requests for logging each image fetch along with breed name and timestamp, and favourites for storing saved dog image URLs with their IDs and timestamps.

### **Render**

Render hosts the Flask application on its free tier. Render watches the GitHub repository for pushes to master and automatically redeploys on each push. Environment variables are configured directly in the Render dashboard, keeping secrets out of the codebase.

## **Branching Model**

The team used a feature branching model. All new work was developed on named feature branches following the format feat/&lt;description&gt;, for example feat/ci-lint-and-tests and feat/health-endpoint. Changes were merged into master via pull requests, allowing for code review before merging. The master branch served as the single production branch, with Render deploying automatically on each merge.

## **Pipeline Design**

The CI/CD pipeline is stored in .github/workflows/ci.yml and runs on every push to master and on every pull request. The pipeline consists of five steps:

- Install dependencies from requirements.txt using pip
- Lint the codebase using ruff to enforce code style
- Test the suite in tests/test_app.py using pytest with coverage reporting via pytest-cov
- Build a Docker image to verify the application containerises correctly
- Publish the Docker image to GitHub Container Registry (GHCR)

The pipeline runs on Ubuntu and Python 3.11. Render handles continuous deployment automatically on every push to master, pulling the latest code and restarting the service.
